title: Slack / Discord — System Design
description: Design a team-chat platform organized around workspaces and channels: many members share channels, messages fan out to everyone in the channel in real time, history is searchable, and access is permission-controlled. The themes are channel fanout (vs 1:1 chat), the workspace/membership/permission model, searchable history, and presence.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Connects to workspaces/channels; sends and receives messages in real time.

nodes.label: Connection Gateway
nodes.description: Routes WebSocket connections to gateway nodes.

nodes.label: WebSocket Gateway
nodes.description: Holds clients' live connections; relays frames.

nodes.label: Channel Service
nodes.description: Permission-checks and persists channel messages, then enqueues them for fanout.

nodes.label: Message Store
nodes.description: Durable messages, sharded by channel; ordered per channel.

nodes.label: Subscription Registry
nodes.description: Which members are subscribed/connected to each channel (routing).

nodes.label: Fanout Queue
nodes.description: Buffers fanout work per channel after the durable commit; queue depth absorbs large-channel bursts and applies backpressure.

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Drains the fanout queue and delivers a channel message to all subscribed members' gateways.

nodes.label: Search Service
nodes.description: Search API boundary: resolves the requester's accessible channels and enforces ACLs, ranking, pagination, and audit before querying the index.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Full-text index over messages, scoped by permissions.

nodes.label: Search Indexer
nodes.description: Indexes messages for search as they're posted.

functional: Organize into workspaces and channels (public/private); join/leave.
Post a message to a channel; all members see it in real time.
Read channel history and unread state per user.
Search messages (scoped to what the user can access).
Presence (online/away) and typing indicators; threads and reactions as message extensions.
nonFunctional: Real-time delivery to all channel members (sub-second).
Reliable + ordered messages within a channel; never lost.
Permission-correct: users only see channels/messages they're allowed to.
Scale to large workspaces and large channels (thousands of members).
Searchable history with low-latency, permission-filtered results.

capacity.label: Active users / sockets
capacity.note: One persistent socket per device; ~5K gateways at ~2K sockets each.

capacity.label: Messages
capacity.note: ~10M users x ~30 messages/user/active-hour, peaked; p95 channel ~50 members, max ~50K.

capacity.label: Fanout writes
capacity.note: Avg ~30 online members/message; an @everyone to a 50K channel is a single 50K-push burst.

capacity.label: Fleet sizing (multipliers)
capacity.note: 10M sockets ÷ 2K/gateway ≈ 5K gateways; 1.5M pushes/sec ÷ 50K pushes/sec/worker ≈ 30 workers, scaling up on queue-depth bursts.

capacity.label: Delivery latency
capacity.note: Push to online members; queue depth is the backpressure signal.

capacity.label: Storage / retention
capacity.note: Retained + searchable; index ingest tracks the message rate (~50K docs/sec).

capacity.label: Search QPS
capacity.note: Low vs message rate; each query ACL-filtered to the user's accessible channels.

capacity.label: Reconnect storm
capacity.note: A drain/crash re-subscribes ~2K clients; jittered backoff + history catch-up absorb it.

api.description: Open a persistent connection; subscribe to the user's channels.

Post a message to a channel; permission-checked, persisted with a per-channel sequence, then enqueued for fanout after the commit (idempotent on clientMsgId). Optional threadId posts a threaded reply.
Served by the search service: resolves the requester's accessible channels from current membership and ACL-filters the index query before returning ranked, paginated hits.
Join (or, for admins, add) a member to a channel; permission-checked, bumps the channel's membership version.
Leave or remove a member; invalidates their live subscription and bumps the membership version so delivery and search stop for them.
Read/catch-up channel history by sequence; the page clients fetch on scrollback or after reconnecting.
Advance the requester's unread cursor (last-read message id) for the channel.
Read a thread: the replies whose thread_id is the root message. Replies are normal messages (persisted, sequenced, fanned out) that also carry a thread_id, so the same post/history path serves them.
Add the requester's reaction to a message (idempotent on message+user+emoji); DELETE removes it. Reaction-count changes fan out to the channel's online members like a lightweight message update.
Ephemeral awareness frames over the live socket (not persisted): typing start/stop and presence change, throttled and auto-expiring.

dataModel.name: messages
dataModel.note: Durable channel messages, sharded by channel; ordered per channel. UNIQUE(channel_id, client_msg_id) makes writes idempotent under retries.

fields.name: thread_id
fields.type: bigint | null (root message_id of the thread)

dataModel.name: channels
dataModel.note: Channels live in a workspace; visibility gates who may discover/join.

fields.name: membership_version
fields.type: bigint (bumped on any membership change; gates stale ACL caches)

dataModel.name: channel_membership
dataModel.note: Who is in a channel + their role + unread cursor; the source of truth for delivery and search scope.

dataModel.name: reactions
dataModel.note: Emoji reactions on a message; one row per (message, user, emoji).

dataModel.name: subscriptions (live)
dataModel.note: Routing: which connected gateways have members of a channel.

patterns.name: Channel-centric fanout
patterns.what: Deliver a posted message to all subscribed members of the channel in real time.

patterns.name: Subscription registry
patterns.what: Track which channels each connected client subscribes to so fanout knows where to push.
patterns.whenToUse: Routing channel messages across a sharded gateway fleet.

patterns.name: Per-channel ordering + history
patterns.what: Assign a per-channel sequence; persist durably so history is ordered and replayable.

patterns.name: Permission model (workspace/channel ACL)
patterns.what: Workspaces contain channels; membership + roles gate read/post/search.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tenant team chat with private channels.

patterns.name: Permission-scoped search
patterns.what: Index messages but filter search results to channels the requester can access.
patterns.whenToUse: Searchable history that must respect access control.

patterns.name: Ephemeral presence
patterns.what: Best-effort online/typing state, separate from the durable message path.

patterns.name: Permission revocation under distributed state
patterns.what: On membership change, invalidate live subscriptions and bump a membership version so delivery and search read current access — no stale cache keeps a removed user connected or searching.
patterns.whenToUse: Private channels where a user can be removed while connected, with cached ACLs in fanout and search.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One Channel, Poll for Messages, No Permissions
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. There's effectively one shared channel: a client POSTs a message, the channel service writes it to a single messages table, and every client polls (GET /messages?since=...) to pull new rows. There's no workspace, no membership, no permissions — everyone sees everything.

It's trivial to build, and that's exactly why it exposes the gaps. Polling is high-latency and floods the store with empty reads — the same problem as any chat. The team-chat-specific gap is bigger: with no membership or permission model, there are no private channels, no way to scope who receives or can search a message, and no notion of which members a post should reach. One flat channel simply doesn't scale to many channels with thousands of members each.

So the baseline motivates the two things that define team chat over 1:1 chat: a workspace/channel/permission model that says who belongs where, and real-time fanout that pushes a posted message to exactly that channel's members.

view.caption: The client sends a message to the channel service, which persists it in order to the single message store.

steps.decisionPrompt: If there's one flat channel everyone polls, what breaks when you add thousands of members, private channels, and permission-scoped search?
whyNow: Establishing the flat-channel polling baseline first makes it obvious why team chat needs both a membership/permission backbone and real-time channel fanout — neither exists yet.

recap.after: One shared channel; messages stored in one table; clients poll for new rows.
recap.newRisk: Polling is high-latency and wasteful, and with no membership/permission model there are no private channels, no scoped delivery, and no scoped search.

traps.trap: Treating team chat as one big chat room with a single message table.
traps.why: Without workspaces, channels, and permissions there's no way to scope delivery or search, and a flat table can't model who belongs to which channel.
traps.instead: Model workspaces/channels/membership first, then fan a posted message out to that channel's members in real time.

steps.title: 2. Workspaces, Channels, and Permissions
description: The organizing model is workspaces (tenants) containing channels (public or private), with users as members. A membership/permission layer governs who can join, read, post in, and search each channel — this is the multi-tenant backbone Slack/Discord add over plain chat.

Every message operation is permission-checked: can this user post here, read this channel? Membership defines the routing target set (who gets a channel's messages) and the search scope (what a user can find).
Get this first: fanout, history, and search all depend on 'who's allowed in this channel'.

view.caption: The channel service permission-checks each message against the membership service, which reads and writes the membership/ACL store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why model permissions/membership before designing message delivery?

concepts.definition: Tenancy + membership + roles that gate read/post/search per channel.
concepts.whyItMatters: Membership defines both delivery targets and search scope — it's foundational, not an add-on.
concepts.example: Private channel: only members route/receive/search its messages.

whyNow: The workspace/channel/permission model is what distinguishes team chat from 1:1; everything else depends on membership.

recap.after: Workspaces/channels with membership + permissions as the backbone.
recap.newRisk: Posting to a channel must reach all members in real time — channel fanout.

steps.title: 3. Channel Fanout over Live Connections
description: Like any chat, clients hold persistent WebSocket connections to gateways. The difference from 1:1: a channel message must reach every member of the channel, not one recipient.

On connect, clients subscribe to their channels in a subscription registry (which gateways hold members of each channel). When a message is posted, the channel service persists it, then the fanout service looks up subscribed members and pushes to their gateways — channel-centric fanout.

Order matters here: persist first, then enqueue for fanout. Because the message is already durable, a fanout retry or worker restart can re-push it without losing or reordering anything — gateways and clients just dedup the occasional duplicate by message id. Fanout is where large channels hurt: one post to a 5,000-member channel is 5,000 pushes. So buffer the work through a queue and push only to online members; offline members read on reconnect. Step 7 makes that queue explicit and bounds it.

view.caption: The client connects through the load balancer and gateway and subscribes channels in the registry; a posted message goes from the channel service through the fanout queue to the fanout service, which looks up subscribers and pushes via gateways.

options.name: Push fanout to online members (default)
options.description: When a message is posted, the fanout service looks up the channel's subscribed members in the registry and pushes it over each online member's live WebSocket.
pros: On post, the fanout service looks up subscribed members and pushes over their live WebSockets — instant delivery.
Channel-centric: one lookup of subscribers drives delivery to everyone online.
Offline members simply catch up from history on reconnect, so push only targets active sockets.
cons: A post to a 5,000-member channel becomes thousands of pushes — fanout is the scaling cost.
Busy large channels amplify load and can melt gateways without buffering.
Requires a subscription registry tracking which gateway holds each member.

view.caption: The channel service enqueues fanout after the durable commit; the fanout service drains the queue, looks up the channel's subscribers in the registry, and pushes the message over each online member's gateway connection.

options.name: Pull / read-on-demand from history
options.description: No per-message fanout: clients fetch new messages from the channel's stored history when they next look, rather than being pushed to.
pros: No per-message fanout work — clients fetch new messages from the channel's history when they look.
Cheap for huge announcement channels where pushing to everyone is wasteful.
No subscription registry or push fan-out to maintain for those channels.
cons: Higher perceived latency: members see a message only when they next poll/scroll.
Polling many channels floods the history store with reads.
Loses the real-time feel that interactive channels need.

view.caption: Clients pull new messages from the channel service's ordered message store on demand, with no per-message fanout.

options.name: Hybrid: push for small channels, pull for huge ones
options.description: A per-channel policy uses push fanout for small interactive channels and pull-from-history for huge announcement channels, switching mode by size.
pros: Interactive channels keep instant push delivery; massive announcement channels switch to pull.
Caps fanout cost where it would explode while preserving real-time UX where it matters.
A per-channel policy lets the system adapt by size and activity.
cons: Two delivery paths to build, test, and reason about.
Need a clear, possibly dynamic threshold for when a channel flips modes.
Mixed semantics (some channels live, some lazy) complicate client logic.

view.caption: Small channels push through the fanout queue, fanout service, and registry while clients pull huge channels directly from the message store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A message is posted to a 5,000-member channel. How does it reach everyone who's online?

concepts.definition: Deliver each channel message to all subscribed members' gateways via a registry of who's subscribed where.
concepts.whyItMatters: Generalizes 1:1 routing to N recipients; the registry makes cross-gateway delivery possible.
concepts.example: Post -> registry: members on gw3, gw7, gw9 -> push to each.

whyNow: Channel fanout is the core real-time difference from WhatsApp; the subscription registry is how it routes across a sharded fleet.

recap.after: Real-time channel fanout to online members.
recap.newRisk: Messages must be durable, ordered, and readable as history — not just pushed live.

flows.name: Post -> persist -> channel fanout to online members
flows.note: A posted message is permission-checked and persisted, then fanned out via the subscription registry to every gateway holding an online member of the channel.

steps.title: 4. Ordered Durable History
description: Unlike ephemeral chat, channel history is first-class: users scroll back, see context, and search. Persist every message durably before acking, assigning a per-channel sequence so history is consistently ordered for everyone.

Shard messages by channel so a channel's history lives together and reads (scrollback, catch-up on reconnect) are efficient. Dedup on a client message id so retries don't duplicate, and track each user's unread cursor (last-read message id) for unread badges.
Offline/returning members read missed messages from the store by sequence — the same persist-then-fanout model where the store is the source of truth and live push is the fast path.

Threads and reactions ride this same model rather than a new path. A threaded reply is just a normal message carrying a thread_id (the root message's id), so it's persisted, sequenced, and fanned out like any other — and reading a thread is simply a filtered history query. A reaction is a tiny separate row keyed by message+user+emoji (idempotent add/remove); its count change fans out to online members as a lightweight update, without re-writing the message.

view.caption: The channel service persists each message in order to the message store and delivers it to the client over the gateway; returning members catch up by reading history after their last-read cursor.

options.name: Per-channel sequence assigned on write (default)
options.description: Every message is persisted before ack and assigned a monotonic per-channel sequence, with history sharded by channel so all members see one consistent order.
pros: Persist every message durably before acking and assign a per-channel sequence, so all members see one consistent order.
Sharding by channel keeps a channel's history together for efficient scrollback and catch-up.
A monotonic per-channel sequence makes 'messages after cursor X' a simple range read.
cons: Assigning the next sequence per channel is a serialization point on the channel's write path.
A very hot channel's sequence counter can become a contention hotspot.
Cross-channel global ordering is not provided (and not needed).

view.caption: The channel service persists each message with a monotonic per-channel sequence to the message store before acking the client.

options.name: Order by server timestamp
options.description: Each message is stamped with the server's timestamp instead of a per-channel counter, so writes avoid a sequence-allocation serialization point.
pros: No per-channel counter — just stamp each message with the server's time.
Writes don't serialize on a sequence allocation, so they scale out freely.
Simple to implement and shard.
cons: Clock skew across servers can reorder near-simultaneous messages.
Ties on identical timestamps need a deterministic tiebreak or members disagree on order.
Catch-up by timestamp is fragile if clocks drift backward.

view.caption: The channel service stamps each message with the server timestamp when writing to the message store, avoiding a sequence allocator.

options.name: Append to a per-channel log (single writer)
options.description: Each channel is a partitioned append-only log with a single writer, so the offset is the order and there is no separate sequence counter.
pros: Each channel is a partitioned append-only log; offset is the order, with no separate counter.
A single writer per channel partition gives clean total order and natural durability.
Consumers (fanout, indexer, catch-up) all read the same ordered offsets.
cons: One writer per channel partition caps that channel's write throughput.
Operating a partitioned log (rebalancing, retention) is heavier infrastructure.
A partition leader failover briefly stalls that channel's writes.

view.caption: The channel service appends each message to a per-channel log where the offset is the order, and clients read from an offset.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do all members see channel messages in the same order, and how does someone catch up after being offline?

concepts.definition: Monotonic per-channel sequence + per-user last-read cursor.
concepts.whyItMatters: Consistent ordering across members and accurate unread/catch-up state.

whyNow: Durable ordered history is what makes channels searchable and catch-up-able; it's the data backbone search builds on.

recap.newRisk: Searching that history must respect permissions and stay fast.

steps.title: 5. Permission-Scoped Search
description: Search is first-class in team chat. As messages are posted, an indexer writes them to a full-text search index. But results must respect access: a user must never find messages from channels they can't access.

Queries go through a search service (an API boundary), never the raw index. The service resolves the requester's accessible channels from membership and filters the query to those channels (or partitions the index by channel and queries only permitted partitions). It's also where ranking, pagination, and audit live. The rule is absolute: never return a hit the user couldn't open.

Index asynchronously (the message store is the truth); search can lag posting by a few seconds, so a just-posted message may not be findable immediately — surface that freshness window rather than promising instant search. Because the filter reads current membership, revocation is immediate: when a user leaves or is removed from a private channel, that channel drops out of their accessible set and stops appearing in results — no per-user index to rebuild. This is the inverted-index + permission-filter pattern applied to chat.

view.caption: The channel service feeds the indexer, which writes the search index; clients query through the search service, which resolves accessible channels from membership and ACL-filters the index query.

options.name: Query-time permission filter (default)
options.description: Messages go into one shared full-text index, and each query resolves the requester's accessible channels from membership and filters results to those channels.
pros: Resolve the requester's accessible channels from membership and filter the search to those channels.
One shared full-text index stays simple to build and operate.
Permission changes take effect immediately — the filter reads current membership.
cons: The accessible-channel set can be large, making the filter clause heavy.
A bug in the filter risks leaking a private-channel hit the user can't open.
Hot users with many channels add filter overhead per query.

view.caption: Messages go into one shared search index; the search service resolves the requester's accessible channels from membership and filters the index query to those channels.

options.name: Partition the index by channel
options.description: The search index is partitioned per channel, so a query only touches the partitions for channels the user can access — no cross-channel leakage by construction.
pros: Query only the partitions for channels the user can access — no cross-channel leakage by construction.
Each channel's index is isolated, so a permission boundary maps to a physical boundary.
Smaller per-query index surface for users in few channels.
cons: Fanning a query across many channel partitions and merging results is more complex.
Huge numbers of small channel indexes add management overhead.
A user in thousands of channels still triggers wide scatter-gather.

view.caption: The indexer writes per-channel partitions of the search index, and a client query only touches the partitions it may access.

options.name: Per-user materialized search index
options.description: Each user has their own materialized index containing only messages they can see, so queries are inherently scoped with no query-time permission filter.
pros: Each user has an index of only what they can see, so queries are fast and inherently scoped.
No permission filter at query time at all.
Great latency for the searching user.
cons: Massive duplication: a popular channel's messages are copied into every member's index.
Membership/permission changes require rebuilding or fixing up many user indexes.
Storage and indexing cost balloon with channel membership size.

view.caption: The indexer fans each message into per-user indexes, so a client queries only its own index with no permission filter.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you let users search history without ever surfacing a message from a private channel they're not in?

concepts.definition: Full-text index filtered to the requester's accessible channels at query time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Search must respect ACLs — a leak here is a serious privacy bug.
concepts.example: Query 'deploy' AND channel IN (user's accessible channels).

whyNow: Searchable history is a defining team-chat feature, and permission-scoping is the correctness twist that 1:1 chat doesn't have.

recap.newRisk: Presence/typing is high-frequency and shouldn't ride the durable path.

traps.trap: Baking permissions into the index at write time instead of filtering at query time.
traps.why: Membership changes constantly; an ACL snapshot frozen into the index goes stale, so a user removed from a private channel can still get hits (a leak) until the index is rebuilt.
traps.instead: Keep one index of message content and filter each query by the requester's current membership (or a version-checked membership cache), so revocation takes effect on the next query.

traps.trap: Promising instant search right after a message is posted.
traps.why: Indexing is async off the durable path, so there's a freshness window of a few seconds where a just-posted message isn't yet findable.
traps.instead: Treat the message store as the source of truth, index asynchronously, and set expectations that search trails posting slightly.

flows.name: Async index, then permission-scoped query
flows.note: Messages are indexed asynchronously off the post path; queries go through the search service, which resolves the requester's accessible channels and ACL-filters the index query.

steps.title: 6. Presence and Typing
description: Awareness — who's online/away, who's typing — is high-frequency and ephemeral. It does not go through the durable message path; presence updates are best-effort and lossy (missing one momentarily is harmless).

Derive presence from connection liveness (heartbeats on the live socket) and broadcast changes to relevant channels/workspaces, throttled. Typing indicators are sent to the channel's online members and expire quickly.
Scope and throttle broadcasts: don't fan out every typing event to a huge channel — cap presence fanout so awareness doesn't dominate traffic.

view.caption: The gateway feeds presence/typing to the presence service, which broadcasts changes back through gateways to clients off the durable path.

options.name: Ephemeral best-effort pub/sub (default)
options.description: Presence and typing are derived from connection heartbeats and broadcast best-effort over pub/sub, never persisted, with broadcasts throttled and scoped.
pros: Presence and typing are derived from connection heartbeats and broadcast best-effort, never persisted.
Missing one update momentarily is harmless, so the durable message path stays unburdened.
Throttling and scoping broadcasts keeps high-frequency awareness cheap.
cons: State is lost on a server failure and must be re-derived from live connections.
Best-effort means brief inconsistencies in who looks online.
Still needs care to not fan out every typing event to a huge channel.

view.caption: Presence and typing flow from the gateway to the presence service, which broadcasts best-effort back through gateways to clients.

options.name: Durable persisted presence path
options.description: Presence and typing updates are written through the durable, reliable message path so they survive restarts and are queryable like any other state.
pros: Presence/typing survive restarts and are queryable like any other state.
Reuses the same reliable delivery path as messages, so nothing is dropped.
Consistent history of status changes if you ever need to audit it.
cons: Persisting extremely high-frequency, low-value updates wastes write capacity.
Couples cheap ephemeral signals to the correctness-critical message store.
Adds latency and load to the durable path for data that's stale in seconds anyway.

view.caption: The client sends status to the presence service, which persists it durably to the message store like any other state.

options.name: Client polling for presence
options.description: Clients periodically query the online/typing state of relevant users from in-memory presence, so there is no push fanout of transient changes.
pros: Clients periodically ask for the online/typing state of relevant users — no push fan-out.
Simple server side: answer a status query from in-memory presence.
Avoids broadcasting every transient change to everyone.
cons: Polling interval trades freshness against request volume.
Typing indicators feel laggy when driven by polling.
Many clients polling many users still generates substantial request load.

view.caption: The client periodically polls the presence service for the current online/typing state, with no push fanout.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should 'user is typing' go through the same durable, persisted path as messages?
whyNow: Presence is a core feature with a distinct (ephemeral, best-effort) consistency profile; separating it keeps the durable path clean and cheap.

concepts.definition: Best-effort, non-durable online/typing state derived from connection liveness.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps awareness cheap and off the durable message path.
concepts.example: Typing event throttled, broadcast to online channel members, auto-expires.

recap.newRisk: Large channels and gateway failures stress fanout and reconnection at scale.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Large Channels and Gateways
description: Large channels make fanout the bottleneck: one message to thousands of members is thousands of pushes, amplified by busy channels. Buffer fanout through the fanout queue (after the durable commit) and drain it with horizontally scalable workers; push only to online members and let others catch up from history on reconnect. Queue depth is the backpressure signal — sustained lag on a channel triggers shedding to pull mode rather than melting gateways, and a dead-letter path isolates poison messages so one bad fanout doesn't stall the channel.

Partition the queue by channel so per-channel ordering is preserved and a busy channel only blocks its own partition. The catch: one @everyone post to a 50K-member channel can still monopolize its own partition. The fix is to fan out in bounded batches — chunk the 50K recipients into many small work items — and round-robin across partitions so a giant channel can't starve small interactive ones. Weighting or rate-limiting per channel keeps head-of-line blocking on a hot partition from delaying everyone. Because the workers are stateless and scale with queue depth, those chunked work items spread a single large fanout across the whole worker pool.

Size the physical fleet from the multipliers. ~10M sockets at ~2K per gateway ≈ ~5K gateways. ~50K msg/sec at an average ~30 online members each ≈ ~1.5M pushes/sec, so at ~50K pushes/sec/worker that's ~30 fanout workers steady-state. A single 50K-member @everyone is a ~50K-push burst on top — the queue absorbs it and the chunked workers drain it over a second or two. Shard messages by channel and the gateway fleet by connection, and keep the subscription registry rebuildable from gateways re-registering. Very large 'announcement' channels can switch to a pull/read model (members fetch rather than receive a push) when fanout cost is too high.

Gateways are thin and replaceable: on failure, clients reconnect to another gateway, re-subscribe, and catch up from the durable store — a brief reconnect, not lost messages. Presence fanout is throttled to avoid amplification.

view.caption: The channel service persists to the store and enqueues fanout; scalable workers drain the queue, read subscribers from the registry, and push via gateways — queue depth absorbs large-channel bursts and signals when to shed to pull mode.

steps.decisionPrompt: An @everyone post hits a 50,000-member channel. What's the cost, and how do you keep it from melting the system?
whyNow: Closing on large-channel fanout + gateway failover shows the design scales where team chat actually hurts (big channels) and degrades gracefully.

recap.after: Buffered fanout, rebuildable routing, reconnect-tolerant, pull for huge channels.
recap.newRisk: Fanout lag on huge channels and reconnect storms — bounded by queuing, pull-mode, and catch-up-from-history.

bottlenecks.issue: Fanout to a very large channel.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Buffer in the fanout queue + scalable workers; watch queue depth/fanout lag and switch huge channels to a pull/read model when sustained.

bottlenecks.issue: Fanout queue lag during a burst (e.g. @everyone).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Backpressure on queue depth, scale workers out, prioritize small channels, and degrade huge channels to pull; dead-letter poison messages so one failure doesn't stall the channel.

bottlenecks.issue: A hot channel's partition head-of-line-blocks small interactive channels.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition the queue by channel (preserves per-channel order), chunk a large fanout into bounded recipient batches, and round-robin / rate-limit across partitions so one giant channel can't starve the rest.
Thin gateways + rebuildable subscription registry + client reconnect with catch-up.

bottlenecks.issue: Reconnect storm after a gateway drain/crash.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Jittered reconnect backoff, spread re-subscribes across the fleet, and serve catch-up from history (not re-fanout) so the store, not the gateways, absorbs the load.

bottlenecks.issue: Search refresh lag and stale permission filters.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Filter search by current membership at query time; expose index-freshness so recent posts may lag, and invalidate cached ACLs on membership change.

failureDrills.scenario: A gateway with many connections crashes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients reconnect to other gateways, re-subscribe, and catch up missed messages from the store.
failureDrills.mitigation: Durable history + rebuildable registry + reconnect with last-read cursor.

failureDrills.scenario: A user is removed from a private channel while they hold a live socket and have cached search permissions.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Delivery stops at the next fanout (subscription is invalidated), the gateway drops their channel subscription, and search stops returning that channel's hits — the user keeps no live access and surfaces no new results from the channel.
failureDrills.mitigation: Membership-change events invalidate the user's subscription in the registry and bump a membership version; fanout and search both read current membership (or a version-checked cache) at fanout/query time, so revocation takes effect without waiting for a reconnect.

failureDrills.scenario: Fanout workers fall behind on a busy 50K-member channel.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Queue depth grows but messages are not lost; online members see added latency, the channel degrades to pull mode, and offline members catch up from history.
failureDrills.mitigation: Buffered fanout queue + autoscaled workers + per-channel pull-mode switch keyed on sustained queue depth.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Team Chat
finalDesign.description: Workspaces contain channels with membership/permissions that gate posting, delivery, and search. Clients hold WebSocket connections to thin gateways and subscribe to their channels in a subscription registry. A posted message is permission-checked, persisted durably with a per-channel sequence (dedup by client message id), then enqueued on the fanout queue after the commit; scalable fanout workers drain the queue and push to all online subscribed members (offline members catch up from history via their last-read cursor). An async indexer powers full-text search, which clients reach through a search service that filters results to their current membership. Presence/typing is ephemeral and best-effort, off the durable path. Membership changes invalidate live subscriptions and bump a membership version so delivery and search stop for a revoked user without waiting for reconnect. Large channels buffer fanout in the queue (degrading to pull on sustained lag), gateways are replaceable, and the subscription registry is rebuildable — so the system scales and reconnects without losing messages.

view.caption: Clients connect through gateways and subscribe channels in the registry; the permission-checked channel service persists ordered history and enqueues fanout, workers drain the queue and push to online subscribers, the search service ACL-filters queries against the indexed history, and presence broadcasts off the durable path.

nonFunctional.how: Fanout queue + scalable workers + pull mode + sharding.
Thin gateways + rebuildable registry + reconnect catch-up.
Invalidate live subscriptions + membership version gating delivery and search.

say: Confirm channel-centric (not 1:1) with workspaces, permissions, and searchable history.
Pin real-time fanout and permission-correctness as the goals.
Note large channels are the scaling challenge.
Tradeoffs: push vs pull for huge channels, search index freshness, presence fanout cost.
Call out the key metrics: fanout queue depth/lag, gateway connection count, search refresh lag, permission-filter failures.
Cover permission revocation across live subscriptions and search.
Mention threads, reactions, and E2E encryption as extensions.

expectations: Uses WebSockets and fans out channel messages.
May miss subscription routing, permission-scoped search, large-channel scaling.
Reasons about large-channel fanout (push vs pull).
Discusses permission-scoped search correctness and presence amplification precisely.

followUps: How do you handle fanout for a 50K-member announcement channel?
How do you keep search results consistent with channel permission changes?
How would you add threads and reactions to the model?
How do you keep presence accurate without flooding large channels?
How would you support cross-workspace shared channels?
